questionText: Compare the numbers 584,219 and 548,219. Which statement correctly explains how the value of the digit 4 changes and why?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can identify the value of any digit in a 6-digit number
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Large Numbers & Place Value: Numbers to 1,000,000, aligned to Students can identify the value of any digit in a 6-digit number.
options: [{"text":"In 584,219, the 4 is worth 4,000. In 548,219, the 4 is worth 40,000. Its value becomes 10 times greater because it moves one place to the left.","correct":true},{"text":"In 584,219, the 4 is worth 4,000. In 548,219, the 4 is worth 40,000. Its value becomes 10 times greater because it moves one place to the right.","correct":false},{"text":"In 584,219, the 4 is worth 400. In 548,219, the 4 is worth 4,000. Its value becomes 10 times greater because it moves one place to the left.","correct":false},{"text":"In 584,219, the 4 is worth 4,000. In 548,219, the 4 is worth 40,000. Its value becomes 40,000 greater because the digit 4 always means 4 ones.","correct":false}]
explanation: In 584,219, the digit 4 is in the thousands place, so its value is 4,000. In 548,219, the digit 4 is in the ten-thousands place, so its value is 40,000. Moving one place to the left makes a digit's value 10 times greater, so the first statement is correct. The second choice has the wrong direction, because moving right makes a value smaller. The third choice starts with the wrong values for the 4. The fourth choice mixes up face value and place value: the digit is 4, but its value depends on its position in the number.

questionText: Look at the number 731,564. Which statement best explains the value of the digit 1 and why another common claim about it is wrong?

summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Large Numbers & Place Value: Numbers to 1,000,000, aligned to Students can identify the value of any digit in a 6-digit number.
options: [{"text":"The 1 is worth 1 because the digit is 1, so place does not matter here.","correct":false},{"text":"The 1 is worth 1,000 because it is in the thousands place, not the ones place.","correct":true},{"text":"The 1 is worth 100 because it is the third digit from the left.","correct":false},{"text":"The 1 is worth 10,000 because it is between 3 and 5.","correct":false}]
explanation: In 731,564, counting from the right gives ones 4, tens 6, hundreds 5, thousands 1, ten-thousands 3, hundred-thousands 7. So the digit 1 is in the thousands place and its value is 1,000. The claim that it is worth only 1 ignores place value. It is not 100, because it is not in the hundreds place, and it is not 10,000, because it is not in the ten-thousands place.

questionText: On a mini safari map, the animal score is 468,352. A note says, "The value of the digit to the right of 8 is 8,000." Which choice gives the best judgment of that note?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can identify the value of any digit in a 6-digit number
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Large Numbers & Place Value: Numbers to 1,000,000, aligned to Students can identify the value of any digit in a 6-digit number.
options: [{"text":"The note is correct because 8 is in the thousands place, so 8,000 is the value named.","correct":false},{"text":"The note is wrong because the digit to the right of 8 is 3, and its value is 300.","correct":true},{"text":"The note is wrong because the digit to the right of 8 is 5, and its value is 50.","correct":false},{"text":"The note is correct because the digit to the right of 8 is still part of 8,352.","correct":false}]
explanation: In 468,352, the 8 is in the thousands place. The digit immediately to the right of 8 is 3. That 3 is in the hundreds place, so its value is 300. The note is wrong because it gives the value of 8 instead of the value of the digit to the right of 8. The digit to the right is not 5, and being part of 8,352 does not change the place value of 3.
